question_html: Monochromatic light of wavelength 589 nm is incident from air on a water surface. What are the wavelength, frequency and speed of (a) reflected, and (b) refracted light? Refractive index of water is 1.33.
question_text: Monochromatic light of wavelength 589 nm is incident from air on a water surface. What are the wavelength, frequency and speed of (a) reflected, and (b) refracted light? Refractive index of water is 1.33.
explanation: Given, {tex}\lambda = 589nm{/tex} {tex}c = 3 \times {10^8}m/s,\mu = 1.33{/tex} For reflected light, Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 589nm = 589 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{589 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 5.09 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} Hence, the speed, frequency, and wavelength of the reflected light are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz, and 589 nm respectively. Frequency of light does not depend on the property of the medium in which it is travelling. Hence, the frequency of the refracted ray in water will be equal to the frequency of the incident or reflected light in air. {tex}v=\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{3\times10^8}{1.33}=2.26\times10^8\mathrm m/\mathrm s{/tex} Wavelength of light in water is given by the relation, {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.26 \times 10^{8}}{5.09 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} {tex}=444.007 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=444.01\;nm{/tex} Hence the speed, frequency and wavelength of refracted light are 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz and 444.01 nm respectively.
solution: Given, {tex}\lambda = 589nm{/tex} {tex}c = 3 \times {10^8}m/s,\mu = 1.33{/tex} For reflected light, Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 589nm = 589 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{589 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 5.09 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} Hence, the speed, frequency, and wavelength of the reflected light are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz, and 589 nm respectively. Frequency of light does not depend on the property of the medium in which it is travelling. Hence, the frequency of the refracted ray in water will be equal to the frequency of the incident or reflected light in air. {tex}v=\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{3\times10^8}{1.33}=2.26\times10^8\mathrm m/\mathrm s{/tex} Wavelength of light in water is given by the relation, {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.26 \times 10^{8}}{5.09 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} {tex}=444.007 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=444.01\;nm{/tex} Hence the speed, frequency and wavelength of refracted light are 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz and 444.01 nm respectively.
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\lambda = 589nm{/tex}
{tex}c = 3 \times {10^8}m/s,\mu = 1.33{/tex}

  1. For reflected light,
    Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 589nm = 589 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex}
    {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{589 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 5.09 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}
    Hence, the speed, frequency, and wavelength of the reflected light are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz, and 589 nm respectively.
  2. Frequency of light does not depend on the property of the medium in which it is travelling. Hence, the frequency of the refracted ray in water will be equal to the frequency of the incident or reflected light in air.
    {tex}v=\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex}
    {tex}v=\frac{3\times10^8}{1.33}=2.26\times10^8\mathrm m/\mathrm s{/tex}
    Wavelength of light in water is given by the relation,
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.26 \times 10^{8}}{5.09 \times 10^{14}}{/tex}
    {tex}=444.007 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=444.01\;nm{/tex}
    Hence the speed, frequency and wavelength of refracted light are 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz and 444.01 nm respectively.

question_html:
In Young's double slit experiment, the slit separation is 1 mm and the screen is 1 m away from the slits. For a monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm, find

  1. the distance of third minima, and
  2. the distance of second maxima,

from the central maxima.
question_text: In Young's double slit experiment, the slit separation is 1 mm and the screen is 1 m away from the slits. For a monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm, find the distance of third minima, and the distance of second maxima, from the central maxima.
explanation: Given distance between two slits d = 1 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m distance of screen D = 1 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500 nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m Using condition for minima x = {tex}\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} n = 3 x = {tex}\left(3-\frac{1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.25 mm Using condition for maxima x = {tex}\frac{n D \lambda}{d}{/tex} n = 2 x = {tex}2 \times \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 mm
solution: Given distance between two slits d = 1 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m distance of screen D = 1 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500 nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m Using condition for minima x = {tex}\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} n = 3 x = {tex}\left(3-\frac{1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.25 mm Using condition for maxima x = {tex}\frac{n D \lambda}{d}{/tex} n = 2 x = {tex}2 \times \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 mm
solution_html:
Given distance between two slits d = 1 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
distance of screen D = 1 m
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500 nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m

  1. Using condition for minima 
    x = {tex}\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    n = 3
    x = {tex}\left(3-\frac{1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.25 mm
  2. Using condition for maxima
    x = {tex}\frac{n D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    n = 2
    x = {tex}2 \times \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 mm

question_html:
What is the effect on the interference fringes in Young’s double-slit experiment due to each of the following operations? Justify your answers.

  1. The screen is moved away from the plane of the slits.
  2. The separation between slits is increased.
  3. The source slit is moved closer to the plane of double slit.
question_text: What is the effect on the interference fringes in Young’s double-slit experiment due to each of the following operations? Justify your answers. The screen is moved away from the plane of the slits. The separation between slits is increased. The source slit is moved closer to the plane of double slit.
explanation: D is Distance between screen to slits, d is distance between the slits, λ is wavelength Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} Since {tex}\beta \propto D{/tex} , the fringe width will increase, as screen is moved away. {tex}\beta \propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex} , therefore fringe width will decrease as the separation between slits is increased. Let s be the width of the source slit and S its distance from plane of two slit. For interference fringes to be distinctly seen, the condition {tex}\frac{s}{S}<\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} should be satisfied, otherwise, the interference patterns produced will overlap.
solution: D is Distance between screen to slits, d is distance between the slits, λ is wavelength Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} Since {tex}\beta \propto D{/tex} , the fringe width will increase, as screen is moved away. {tex}\beta \propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex} , therefore fringe width will decrease as the separation between slits is increased. Let s be the width of the source slit and S its distance from plane of two slit. For interference fringes to be distinctly seen, the condition {tex}\frac{s}{S}<\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} should be satisfied, otherwise, the interference patterns produced will overlap.
solution_html:
1.  D is Distance between screen to slits,  d is distance between the slits, λ is wavelength

    Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} 
    Since {tex}\beta \propto D{/tex}, the fringe width will increase, as screen is moved away.
  2. {tex}\beta \propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex}, therefore fringe width will decrease as the separation between slits is increased.
  3. Let s be the width of the source slit and S its distance from plane of two slit. For interference fringes to be distinctly seen, the condition
    {tex}\frac{s}{S}<\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} 
    should be satisfied, otherwise, the interference patterns produced will overlap.

question_text: A plane wavefront is propagating from a rarer into a denser medium. Use Huygens principle to show the refracted wavefront and verify Snell's law.
explanation: Let XY be interface and c 1 and c 2 are velocity of light in rarer and denser medium respectively. then, {tex}\mu=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} is refractive index of medium 2 with respect to medium 1. According to Huygens principle, every point of plane wavefront AB acts as a source of secondary wavelet. Let the secondary wavelets from B strikes XY at A' in t seconds BA' = c 1 {tex}\times{/tex} t Similarly from A secondary wavelet travels in denser medium with velocity c 2 AB' = c 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B sin i = {tex}\frac{B A^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_1 t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B' sin r = {tex}\frac{A B^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_2 \times t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{c_1}{c_2}=u=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This proves snell's law of refraction.

question_html: Define the term wavefront. State Huygen's principle. Consider a plane wavefront incident on a thin convex lens. Draw a proper diagram to show how the incident wavefront traverses through the lens and after refraction focusses on the focal point of the lens, giving the shape of the emergent wavefront.
question_text: Define the term wavefront. State Huygen's principle. Consider a plane wavefront incident on a thin convex lens. Draw a proper diagram to show how the incident wavefront traverses through the lens and after refraction focusses on the focal point of the lens, giving the shape of the emergent wavefront.
explanation: When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around it begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront. Huygens' principle: Every point on a wave-front may be considered a source of secondary spherical wavelets which spread out in the forward direction at the speed of light. The new wave-front is the tangential surface to all of these secondary wavelets. ​​​​​ Now when a plane wavefront (parallel rays) is incident on a thin convex lens, the emergent rays are focused on the focal point of the lens. Thus the shape of emerging wavefront is spherical.

Thus the shape of emerging wavefront is spherical.

question_html:
1. When monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media, the reflected and refracted light both have the same frequency as the incident frequency. Explain why?
  2. When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does the reduction in speed imply a reduction in the energy carried by the light wave?
  3. In the wave picture of light, intensity of light is determined by the square of the amplitude of the wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light.
question_text: When monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media, the reflected and refracted light both have the same frequency as the incident frequency. Explain why? When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does the reduction in speed imply a reduction in the energy carried by the light wave? In the wave picture of light, intensity of light is determined by the square of the amplitude of the wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light.
explanation: Reflection and refraction arise through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators, which take up the frequency of the external agency (light) causing forced oscillations. The frequency of light emitted by a charged oscillator equals its frequency of oscillation. Thus, the frequency of scattered light equals the frequency of incident light. No, Energy carried by a wave depends on the amplitude of the wave, not on the speed of wave propagation. For a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by the number of photons crossing an unit area per unit time.

  2. No, Energy carried by a wave depends on the amplitude of the wave, not on the speed of wave propagation.
  3. For a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by the number of photons crossing an unit area per unit time.

question_text: In a diffraction pattern due to a single slit, how will the angular width of central maximum change, if Orange light is used in place of green light, the screen is moved closer to the slit, the slit width is decreased? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: angular width of central maxima of a single slit diffraction is given as {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases (orange light has greater wave length) diffraction angle 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} will also increase. Increasing or decreasing closeness of screen and slit does not affect angular width. If a (slit width) decreases, 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} will increase as {tex}2 \theta \propto \frac {1}{a}{/tex}

solution_html:
angular width of central maxima of a single slit diffraction is given as  {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}

  1. As {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases (orange light has greater wave length) diffraction angle 2{tex}\theta{/tex} will also increase.
  2. Increasing or decreasing closeness of screen and slit does not affect angular width.
  3. If a (slit width) decreases, 2{tex}\theta{/tex} will increase as {tex}2 \theta \propto \frac {1}{a}{/tex}

question_html: Using Huygen's principle, for a plane wavefront incident on a plane reflecting surface, draw the reflected wavefront. Hence prove laws of reflection.
question_text: Using Huygen's principle, for a plane wavefront incident on a plane reflecting surface, draw the reflected wavefront. Hence prove laws of reflection.
explanation: Consider a plane Mirror m 1 m 2 Let AB is the plane wavefront incident on the mirror such that {tex}\angle{/tex} BAA' = {tex}\angle{/tex} i 1, 2, 3 are corresponding incident rays perpendicular to AB. Let the secondary wavelet from B strike mirror at A' in t seconds. As according to Huygen's principle A ' B ' acts as reflected wavefront. BA' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t c = velocity of light similarly secondary wavelet travel in t time from A. AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t Thus A'B' is the secondary wavefront after t second. Hence A'B' is the true reflected wavefront. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} s AA'B and AA'B' BA' = AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t {tex}\angle{/tex} B = {tex}\angle{/tex} B' = 90 o AA' is common and thus {tex}\triangle{/tex} s are congruent {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} r first law of reflection Also, incident wavefront AB, the reflected wavefront A'B' and reflecting surface m 1 m 2 all are perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Therefore, incident ray, normal to the mirror m 1 m 2 and reflected ray all lie in the plane of the paper. This is second law of reflection.

1, 2, 3 are corresponding incident rays perpendicular to AB.
 Let the secondary wavelet from B strike mirror at A' in t seconds. As according to Huygen's principle

A'B'  acts as reflected wavefront.

BA' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t
c = velocity of light
similarly secondary wavelet travel in t time from A.
AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t

{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}B' = 90o

explanation: {tex} y_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex} {tex} y_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex} According to Principle of Superposition {tex} {y} =y_1+y_2 {/tex} {tex} =a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)] {/tex} {tex} =2 a \cos \phi / 2 \cos (\omega t+\phi / 2) {/tex} {tex} {y}=\operatorname{Acos}(\omega {t}+\phi / 2) {/tex} where, {tex}{A}=2 a \cos \phi / 2{/tex} {tex} I=k A^2 {/tex} {tex} I=k\left(4 a^2 \cos ^2 \phi / 2\right) {/tex} {tex} I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex} Maximum value {tex}I=4 I_0{/tex} Minimum value {tex}I=0{/tex} Position of first order minimum {tex} y =\frac{n \lambda D}{a} {/tex} {tex} y_1 =\frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{3 \times 10^{-3}}=3 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex} Position of second order maximum {tex} y_n=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda D}{2 a} {/tex} {tex} n=2, y_2 =\frac{5 \lambda D}{2 a} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}=7.5 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex}

solution_html:
1. {tex} y_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex}
    {tex} y_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex}
    According to Principle of Superposition
    {tex} {y} =y_1+y_2 {/tex}
    {tex} =a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)] {/tex}
    {tex} =2 a \cos \phi / 2 \cos (\omega t+\phi / 2) {/tex}
    {tex} {y}=\operatorname{Acos}(\omega {t}+\phi / 2) {/tex}
    where, {tex}{A}=2 a \cos \phi / 2{/tex}
    {tex} I=k A^2 {/tex}
    {tex} I=k\left(4 a^2 \cos ^2 \phi / 2\right) {/tex}
    {tex} I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex}
    Maximum value {tex}I=4 I_0{/tex}

    1. Position of first order minimum
      {tex} y =\frac{n \lambda D}{a} {/tex}
      {tex} y_1 =\frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{3 \times 10^{-3}}=3 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex}
    2. Position of second order maximum

question_text: What are coherent sources? Why are they necessary for observing a sustained interference pattern? Lights from two independent sources are not coherent. Explain. How far apart will adjacent bright interference fringes be on the screen? Find the angular width (in degree) of the first bright fringe.
explanation: If the phase difference between the displacement produced by each of the wave from two sources does not change with time then two sources are said to be coherent. Two independent sources will never be coherent because phase difference between them will not be constant. question_text: In Young's double slit experiment, deduce the condition for (a) constructive and (b) destructive interference at a point on the screen. Draw a graph showing a variation of intensity in the interference pattern against position x on the screen.
explanation: Suppose S 1 and S 2 are two fine slits, a small distance d apart. They are illuminated by a strong source S of monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda {/tex} . MN is a screen at a distance D from the slits. Consider a point P at a distance y from 0, the centre of the screen. The path difference between two waves arriving at point Pis equal to S 2 P - S 1 P. Now, (S 2 P) 2 -(S 1 P) 2 {tex}= \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y + \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y - \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = 2 y d{/tex} Thus, {tex}S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P = \frac { 2 y d } { S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P }{/tex} But {tex}S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P \approx 2 D{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P \approx \frac { d y } { D }{/tex} For constructive interference (Bright fringes) Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = n \lambda{/tex} , where, n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...... {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { n D \lambda } { d }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ....] For destructive interference (Dark fringes) Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = ( 2 n - 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} The distribution of intensity in Young's double slit experiment is as shown below

MN is a screen at a distance D from the slits. Consider a point P at a distance y from 0, the centre of the screen. The path difference between two waves arriving at point Pis equal to S 2 P - S 1 P. Now, (S 2 P) 2 -(S 1 P) 2 {tex}= \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y + \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y - \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = 2 y d{/tex} Thus, {tex}S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P = \frac { 2 y d } { S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P }{/tex} But {tex}S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P \approx 2 D{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P \approx \frac { d y } { D }{/tex} For constructive interference (Bright fringes) Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = n \lambda{/tex} , where, n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...... {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { n D \lambda } { d }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ....] For destructive interference (Dark fringes) Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = ( 2 n - 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} The distribution of intensity in Young's double slit experiment is as shown below
solution_html:
Suppose S1 and S2 are two fine slits, a small distance d apart. They are illuminated by a strong source S of monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda {/tex}. MN is a screen at a distance D from the slits.

Consider a point P at a distance y from 0, the centre of the screen.
The path difference between two waves arriving at point Pis equal to S2P - S1P.
Now, (S2P)2 -(S1P)2
{tex}= \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y + \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y - \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = 2 y d{/tex}
Thus, {tex}S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P = \frac { 2 y d } { S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P }{/tex}
But {tex}S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P \approx 2 D{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P \approx \frac { d y } { D }{/tex}

  1. For constructive interference (Bright fringes)
    Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = n \lambda{/tex} , where,
    n = 0, 1, 2, 3,......
    {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { n D \lambda } { d }{/tex} [{tex}\because {/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ....]
  2. For destructive interference (Dark fringes)
    Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = ( 2 n - 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex}

  2. From {tex}{I}^{{st}}{/tex} surface, Refraction is from rarer to denser medium and object is at {tex}\infty{/tex}
    {tex}{n}_{1}=1, {n}_{2} =1.5, {R}=15 {~cm}, {u}=\infty{/tex}

    {tex}\frac{{n}_{1}}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_{2}}{{u}} =\frac{{n}_{1}-{n}_{2}}{{R}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}-\frac{1.5}{15} =\frac{1-1.5}{-15}{/tex}
    {tex}{v} =7.5 {~cm}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\sin {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} {tex}\sin {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{c} / {n}_{1}}{{c} / {n}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{n}_{2}}{{n}_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}{n}_{1} \sin {i}={n}_{2} \sin {r}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex} {tex}u \lt 0, f \gt 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{(-u)}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}} \text { is positive }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {v} \text { is positive } \Rightarrow \text { virtual image }{/tex}

1. {tex}\sin {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex}
    {tex}\sin {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{c} / {n}_{1}}{{c} / {n}_{2}}{/tex}

These wavelets emanating from the wavefront are usually referred to as secondary wavelets and if we draw a common tangent to all these spheres, we obtain the new position of the wavefront at a later time. question_html:
1. A monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident normally on a narrow slit of width 'a' to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed at a distance D from the slit. With the help of a relevant diagram, deduce the conditions for obtaining maxima and minima on the screen.
  2. Use these conditions to show that angular width of central maximum is twice the angular width of secondary maximum.
  3. Explain why the maxima at {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} become weaker and weaker with increasing n.
question_text: A monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident normally on a narrow slit of width 'a' to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed at a distance D from the slit. With the help of a relevant diagram, deduce the conditions for obtaining maxima and minima on the screen. Use these conditions to show that angular width of central maximum is twice the angular width of secondary maximum. Explain why the maxima at {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} become weaker and weaker with increasing n.
explanation: In a single-slit diffraction experiment, monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} passes through a slit of width {tex}a{/tex} , producing a diffraction pattern on a screen placed at a distance {tex}D{/tex} . Minima condition: The minima occur when the path difference between light from the top and bottom of the slit is an integer multiple of the wavelength, causing destructive interference. This occurs at angles {tex}\theta{/tex} given by: {tex} a \sin \theta=m \lambda \text { for } m= \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \ldots{/tex} Maxima condition: Maxima do not occur at regular intervals. However, they can be approximated by: {tex} a \sin \theta=\left(m+\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda \text { for } m=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots{/tex} A diagram would show a central bright fringe at {tex}\theta=0{/tex} and alternating dark and bright fringes. The condition for first minimum on the screen is a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of the central fringe on the screen = 2 {tex}\theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular width of the first secondary diffraction fringes = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} Hence the angular width of central fringe is twice the angular width of first secondary fringe. Maxima become weaker and weaker with increasing n This is because the effective part of the wavefront, contributing to the maxima, becomes smaller and smaller, with the increasing n.

solution_html:
1. In a single-slit diffraction experiment, monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} passes through a slit of width {tex}a{/tex}, producing a diffraction pattern on a screen placed at a distance {tex}D{/tex}.
    Minima condition:
    The minima occur when the path difference between light from the top and bottom of the slit is an integer multiple of the wavelength, causing destructive interference. This occurs at angles {tex}\theta{/tex} given by:
    {tex} a \sin \theta=m \lambda \text { for } m= \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \ldots{/tex}
    Maxima condition:
    Maxima do not occur at regular intervals. However, they can be approximated by:
    {tex} a \sin \theta=\left(m+\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda \text { for } m=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots{/tex}
    A diagram would show a central bright fringe at {tex}\theta=0{/tex} and alternating dark and bright fringes.
  2. The condition for first minimum on the screen is
    a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    The angular width of the central fringe on the screen = 2 {tex}\theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
    Angular width of the first secondary diffraction fringes = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    Hence the angular width of central fringe is twice the angular width of first secondary fringe.
  3. Maxima become weaker and weaker with increasing n This is because the effective part of the wavefront, contributing to the maxima, becomes smaller and smaller, with the increasing n.

question_text: Light consisting of two wavelengths 600 nm and 480 nm is used to obtain interference fringes in a double slit experiment. The screen is placed 1.0 m away from slits which are 1.0 nm apart. Calculate the distance of the third bright fringe on the screen from the central maximum for wavelength 600 nm . Find the least distance from the central maximum where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide. Draw the variation of intensity with angle of diffraction in single slit diffraction pattern. Write the expression for value of angle corresponding to zero intensity locations. In what way diffraction of light waves differs from diffraction of sound waves?

1. Figure shows the geometry of path differences for diffraction by a single slit of width a. Give appropriate 'reasoning' to explain why the intensity of light is
    1. maximum at the central point C on the screen.
    2. (nearly) zero for point P on the screen when {tex}\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}

  2. Diffraction defines the limit of the ray optics. Give a brief explanation of this statement.

Diffraction defines the limit of the ray optics. Give a brief explanation of this statement.
explanation: At the central points C( {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o ), the wavelets from the corresponding parts of the two halves LM and MN of the slit have zero path difference i.e., they are in same phase. This gives maximum intensity at C. The path difference between the extreme rays from L and N is p = NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = a {tex}\theta{/tex} [For small {tex}\theta{/tex} , sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} ] Let a {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Then the path difference between any two corresponding points M 1 and M 2 of the two halves of the slit will be p = {tex}\frac{a \theta}{2}=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} This means the contributions from M 1 and M 2 are 180 o out of phase and cancel in the direction {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda} {a}{/tex} . Contributions from the two halves LM and MN cancel each other. Net intensity at P will be zero. {tex}\therefore \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} is the condition of first minimum. This is the half angular width of the central maximum. Total angular width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Linear width of the central maximum on the screen at distance D = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} . When size of aperture a >> {tex}\lambda{/tex} , we can ignore diffraction effects. Then the light appears to travel in straight lines and ray optics becomes valid. Since for the validity of ray optics a >> {tex}\lambda{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} << a or {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0, it implies that ray optics is a limiting case of wave optics.

explanation: Huygen’s principle Each point of the wavefront is the source of a secondary disturbance and the wavelets emanating from these points spread out in all directions with the speed of the wave. These wavelets emanating from the wavefront are usually referred to as secondary wavelets, a common tangent to all these spheres gives the new position of the wavefront at a later time. Verification of law of reflection In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AEC & {tex}\triangle{/tex} CBA EC = AB (c x t each) {tex}\angle{/tex} AEC = {tex}\angle{/tex} CBA (90 o each) AC = AC (common side) By RHS congruency {tex}\triangle \mathrm{AEC} \cong \Delta \mathrm{CBA}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \angle i=\angle r{/tex} Hence the law of reflection is verified. m = +3, f = -12 cm, u = ? m = {tex}-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = -3u using mirror formula {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{-3 u}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-12}{/tex} u = -8 cm Hence the distance of the object from the mirror is 8 cm

    Verification of law of reflection
    In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEC & {tex}\triangle{/tex}CBA
    EC = AB (c x t each)
    {tex}\angle{/tex}AEC = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBA (90o each)
    AC = AC (common side)
    By RHS congruency {tex}\triangle \mathrm{AEC} \cong \Delta \mathrm{CBA}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \angle i=\angle r{/tex}
    Hence the law of reflection is verified.
  2. m = +3, f = -12 cm, u = ?
    m = {tex}-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = -3u
    using mirror formula
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{-3 u}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-12}{/tex}
    u = -8 cm
    Hence the distance of the object from the mirror is 8 cm

question_text: Describe briefly how a diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen due to a single narrow slit illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. Hence, obtain the conditions for the angular width of secondary maxima and secondary minima. The distance between the slit and the screen is 1.5m. Calculate the separation between the positions of first maxima of the diffraction pattern obtained in the two cases.
explanation: A single narrow slit is illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. The diffraction pattern is obtained on the screen placed in front of the slits. There is a central bright region called as central maximum. All the waves reaching this region are in phase hence the intensity is maximum. On both side of central maximum, there are alternate dark and bright regions, the intensity becoming weaker away from the center. The intensity at any point P on the screen depends on the path difference between the waves arising from different parts of the wave-front at the slit. Diffraction of light at a single slit A parallel beam of light with a plane wavefront WW' is made to fall on a single slit AB. As width of the slit AB = dis of the order of wavelength of light, therefore, diffraction occurs on passing through the slit. The wavelets from the single wavefront reach the centre O on the screen in same phase and hence, interfere constructively to give central maximum (bright fringe). The diffraction pattern obtained on the screen consists of a central bright band having alternate dark and weak bright band of decreasing intensity on both sides. Consider a point P on the screen at which wavelets travelling in a direction making an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with CO are brought to focus by the lens. The wavelets from points A and B will have a path difference equal to BN. From the right angled {tex}\Delta A N B{/tex} , we have BN = AB sin {tex}\theta {/tex} or BN = d sin {tex}\theta {/tex} . To establish the condition for secondary minima, the slit is divided into 2,4,6... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from parts such that corresponding wavelets from successive regions interfere with path difference {tex}\lambda / 2{/tex} or for nth secondary minlmum.the slit can be divided into 2n equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary minimum, path difference= {tex}= d \sin \theta _ { n } = n \lambda{/tex} . or {tex}\sin \theta _ { n } = \frac { n \lambda } { d } ( n = 1,2,3 , \dots ){/tex} To establish the condition for secondary maxima, the slit is divided into 3,5,7... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from alternate regions interfere with path difference of A./2 or for nth secondary maximum, the slit can be divided into (2n+ 1)equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary maximum {tex}d \sin \theta _ { n } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3,...) For {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 590 \mathrm { mm }{/tex} Location of 1 maxima {tex}y _ { 1 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex} If {tex}n = 1 \Rightarrow y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex} For {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 596 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} Location of III maxima {tex}y _ { 2 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex} , if n = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Path difference {tex}= y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D } { 2 d } \left( \lambda _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 1 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 1.5 } { 2 \times 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } ( 596 - 590 ) \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}= 6.75 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
solution: A single narrow slit is illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. The diffraction pattern is obtained on the screen placed in front of the slits. There is a central bright region called as central maximum. All the waves reaching this region are in phase hence the intensity is maximum. On both side of central maximum, there are alternate dark and bright regions, the intensity becoming weaker away from the center. The intensity at any point P on the screen depends on the path difference between the waves arising from different parts of the wave-front at the slit. Diffraction of light at a single slit A parallel beam of light with a plane wavefront WW' is made to fall on a single slit AB. As width of the slit AB = dis of the order of wavelength of light, therefore, diffraction occurs on passing through the slit. The wavelets from the single wavefront reach the centre O on the screen in same phase and hence, interfere constructively to give central maximum (bright fringe). The diffraction pattern obtained on the screen consists of a central bright band having alternate dark and weak bright band of decreasing intensity on both sides. Consider a point P on the screen at which wavelets travelling in a direction making an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with CO are brought to focus by the lens. The wavelets from points A and B will have a path difference equal to BN. From the right angled {tex}\Delta A N B{/tex} , we have BN = AB sin {tex}\theta {/tex} or BN = d sin {tex}\theta {/tex} . To establish the condition for secondary minima, the slit is divided into 2,4,6... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from parts such that corresponding wavelets from successive regions interfere with path difference {tex}\lambda / 2{/tex} or for nth secondary minlmum.the slit can be divided into 2n equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary minimum, path difference= {tex}= d \sin \theta _ { n } = n \lambda{/tex} . or {tex}\sin \theta _ { n } = \frac { n \lambda } { d } ( n = 1,2,3 , \dots ){/tex} To establish the condition for secondary maxima, the slit is divided into 3,5,7... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from alternate regions interfere with path difference of A./2 or for nth secondary maximum, the slit can be divided into (2n+ 1)equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary maximum {tex}d \sin \theta _ { n } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3,...) For {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 590 \mathrm { mm }{/tex} Location of 1 maxima {tex}y _ { 1 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex} If {tex}n = 1 \Rightarrow y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex} For {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 596 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} Location of III maxima {tex}y _ { 2 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex} , if n = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Path difference {tex}= y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D } { 2 d } \left( \lambda _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 1 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 1.5 } { 2 \times 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } ( 596 - 590 ) \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}= 6.75 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}

1. A single narrow slit is illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. The diffraction pattern is obtained on the screen placed in front of the slits. There is a central bright region called as central maximum. All the waves reaching this region are in phase hence the intensity is maximum. On both side of central maximum, there are alternate dark and bright regions, the intensity becoming weaker away from the center. The intensity at any point P on the screen depends on the path difference between the waves arising from different parts of the wave-front at the slit.
    Diffraction of light at a single slit A parallel beam of light with a plane wavefront WW' is made to fall on a single slit AB. As width of the slit AB = dis of the order of wavelength of light, therefore, diffraction occurs on passing through the slit.

    The wavelets from the single wavefront reach the centre O on the screen in same phase and hence, interfere constructively to give central maximum (bright fringe).
    The diffraction pattern obtained on the screen consists of a central bright band having alternate dark and weak bright band of decreasing intensity on both sides.
    Consider a point P on the screen at which wavelets travelling in a direction making an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with CO are brought to focus by the lens. The wavelets from points A and B will have a path difference equal to BN.
    From the right angled {tex}\Delta A N B{/tex}, we have BN = AB sin{tex}\theta {/tex} or BN = d sin{tex}\theta {/tex}.
    To establish the condition for secondary minima, the slit is divided into 2,4,6... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from parts such that corresponding wavelets from successive regions interfere with path difference {tex}\lambda / 2{/tex} 
    or for nth secondary minlmum.the slit can be divided into 2n equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary minimum, path difference= {tex}= d \sin \theta _ { n } = n \lambda{/tex}.
    or {tex}\sin \theta _ { n } = \frac { n \lambda } { d } ( n = 1,2,3 , \dots ){/tex}
    To establish the condition for secondary maxima, the slit is divided into 3,5,7... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from alternate regions interfere with path difference of A./2 or for nth secondary maximum, the slit can be divided into (2n+ 1)equal parts.
    Hence, for nth secondary maximum
    {tex}d \sin \theta _ { n } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3,...)
  2. For {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 590 \mathrm { mm }{/tex}
    Location of 1 maxima {tex}y _ { 1 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex}
    If {tex}n = 1 \Rightarrow y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex}
    For {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 596 \mathrm { nm }{/tex}
    Location of III maxima 
    {tex}y _ { 2 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex}, if n = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Path difference {tex}= y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D } { 2 d } \left( \lambda _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 1 } \right){/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 1.5 } { 2 \times 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } ( 596 - 590 ) \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex}
    {tex}= 6.75 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}

1. Derive the relation a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} for the first minimum of the diffraction pattern produced due to a single slit of width a using light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
  2. State with reason, how the linear width of central maximum will be affected if (i) monochromatic yellow light is replaced with red light, and (ii) distance between the slit and the screen is increased.
  3. Using the monochromatic light of same wavelength in the experimental set-up of the diffraction pattern as well as in the interference pattern where the slit separation is 1 mm, 10 interference fringes are found to be within the central maximum of the diffraction pattern. Determine the width of the single slit, if the screen is kept at the same distance from the slit in the two cases.

Using the monochromatic light of same wavelength in the experimental set-up of the diffraction pattern as well as in the interference pattern where the slit separation is 1 mm, 10 interference fringes are found to be within the central maximum of the diffraction pattern. Determine the width of the single slit, if the screen is kept at the same distance from the slit in the two cases.
explanation: From diagram path difference between the waves from L and N = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} When first minimum is obtained at P then path difference {tex}=\lambda{/tex} [imagine the slit be divided into two halves, for each wavelets from first half of the slit has a corresponding wavelet from second half of the slit differing by a path of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} and cancel each other] Condition for first minimum {tex}\therefore \lambda=\mathrm{a} \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{2 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} As we know that wave length {tex}(\lambda){/tex} of red light is more than yellow light. {tex}\therefore \lambda_{\text {red }}>\lambda_{\text {yellow }}{/tex} So, {tex}\therefore \beta_{\text {red }}>\beta_{\text {yellow }}{/tex} Hence, the linear width of the central maximum will increase if monochromatic yellow light is replaced with the red light. If the distance between the slit and screen (d) is increased then also the linear width of the central maximum will increase. {tex}10 \frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{d}}=2 \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{5}{/tex} = 0.2 mm

A ray of light incident normally on one of the faces of a right angled isosceles prism is found to be totally reflected as shown.

  1. What is the minimum value of the refractive index of the material of the prism?
  2. When the prism is immersed in water, trace the path of the emergent ray for the same incident ray indicating the values of all the angles. ({tex}\mu{/tex} of water = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}).
question_text: A ray of light incident normally on one of the faces of a right angled isosceles prism is found to be totally reflected as shown. What is the minimum value of the refractive index of the material of the prism? When the prism is immersed in water, trace the path of the emergent ray for the same incident ray indicating the values of all the angles. ( {tex}\mu{/tex} of water = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} ).
explanation: ABC is the section of the prism, B is a right angle. A and C are equal angles i.e. A = C = 45°. The ray PQ is normally incident on the face AB. Hence it is normally refracted and the ray QR strikes the face AC at an angle of incidence 45°. It is given that the ray does not undergo refraction but is totally reflected at the face AC. This gives a maximum value for the critical angle as 45°. sin C = sin 45° {tex} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} in the limit Since {tex}\mu = \frac{1}{{\sin C}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} or, {tex}\mu = \frac{1}{{\sin 45}} {/tex} {tex} or \space \mu_\min = \sqrt 2{/tex} The minimum value of refractive index {tex} = \sqrt 2 {/tex} . When the prism is immersed in water the critical angle for the glass water interface is given by {tex}\sin {C_1} = \frac{{4/3}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{4}{{3\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} C 1 = 70.53° The angle of incidence at R continues to be 45° and since 45° < 70.53°, refraction taking place and the refracted ray is RS. The angle of refraction r is given by {tex}{\mu _g}\sin i = {\mu _w}\sin r{/tex} {tex}g{\mu _\omega } = \frac{{\sin i}}{{\sin r}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\mu _\omega }}}{{{\mu _g}}} = \frac{{\sin i}}{{\sin r}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt 2 \sin 45^\circ = \frac{4}{3}\sin r{/tex} {tex}\sin r = \frac{{3\sqrt 2 }}{4}\sin 45^\circ{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{3\sqrt 2 }}{4} \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}r = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{4} = 48^\circ 36'{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The angle of refraction in water = {tex}48^\circ 36'{/tex}

question_html: A narrow monochromatic beam of light of intensity I is incident on a glass plate. Another identical glass plate is kept close to the first one and parallel to it. Each plate reflects 25% of the incident light and transmits the remaining. Calculate the ratio of minimum and maximum intensity in the interference pattern formed by two beams obtained after reflection from each plate.
question_text: A narrow monochromatic beam of light of intensity I is incident on a glass plate. Another identical glass plate is kept close to the first one and parallel to it. Each plate reflects 25% of the incident light and transmits the remaining. Calculate the ratio of minimum and maximum intensity in the interference pattern formed by two beams obtained after reflection from each plate.
explanation: Let I be the intensity of beam 1 incident on the first glass plate. Each plate reflects 25% of light incident on it and transmits 75%. Therefore, I 1 = I; I 2 = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{I}{4}{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{75}{100}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} I I 4 = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex} I I 5 = {tex}\frac{75}{100}{/tex} I 4 = {tex}\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{3}{16}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{9}{64}{/tex} I {tex}\therefore{/tex} Amplitude ratio of beams 2 and 5 is r = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{I_{2}}{I_{5}}}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{4} \times \frac{64}{9 I}}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\min }}{I_{\max }}=\left[\frac{r-1}{r+1}\right]^{2}=\left[\frac{\frac{4}{3}-1}{\frac{4}{3}+1}\right]^{2}=\frac{1}{49}{/tex} = 1 : 49

Therefore,

1. Write two points of difference between an interference pattern and a diffraction pattern.
    1. A ray of light incident on face AB of an equilateral glass prism, shows minimum deviation of 30o. Calculate the speed of light through the prism.
    2. Find the angle of incidence at face AB so that the emergent ray grazes along the face AC.
question_text: Write two points of difference between an interference pattern and a diffraction pattern. A ray of light incident on face AB of an equilateral glass prism, shows minimum deviation of 30 o . Calculate the speed of light through the prism. Find the angle of incidence at face AB so that the emergent ray grazes along the face AC.
explanation: The interference pattern has a number of equally spaced bright and dark bands. The diffraction pattern has a central bright maximum which is twice as wide as the other maxima. The intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre, on either side. We calculate the interference pattern by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit. {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{90}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{60}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{\frac1 2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} Here A = 60 o , {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30 o . Now {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex} : {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 the speed of light through the prism is 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 n = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin c} {/tex} (for face AC) {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin c = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = sin 45 o {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 45 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle \mathrm{APQ}{/tex} = 180 o - (60 o + 45 o ) = 75 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = 90 o - 75 o = 15 o i = ? n = {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 15^{\prime \prime}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin i = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \sin 15^{\prime \prime}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2588

The intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre, on either side. We calculate the interference pattern by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit. {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{90}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{60}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{\frac1 2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} Here A = 60 o , {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30 o . Now {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex} : {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 the speed of light through the prism is 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 n = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin c} {/tex} (for face AC) {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin c = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = sin 45 o {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 45 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle \mathrm{APQ}{/tex} = 180 o - (60 o + 45 o ) = 75 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = 90 o - 75 o = 15 o i = ? n = {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 15^{\prime \prime}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin i = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \sin 15^{\prime \prime}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2588
solution_html:
1. The interference pattern has a number of equally spaced bright and dark bands. The diffraction pattern has a central bright maximum which is twice as wide as the other maxima. The intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre, on either side.
    2. We calculate the interference pattern by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit.
    1. {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{90}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{60}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{\frac1 2}{/tex}
      = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
      Here A = 60o, {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 30o.
      Now {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex}:
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1
      the speed of light through the prism is 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1


    2. n = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin c} {/tex} (for face AC)
      {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin c}{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin c = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = sin 45o

1. Use Huygen's geometrical construction to show how a plane wavefront at t = 0 propagates and produces a wavefront at a later time.
  2. Verify, using Huygen's principle, Snell's law of refraction of a plane wave propagating from a denser to a rarer medium.
  3. Illustrate with the help of diagrams the action of
    1. convex lens and
    2. concave mirror, on a plane wavefront incident on it.
question_text: Use Huygen's geometrical construction to show how a plane wavefront at t = 0 propagates and produces a wavefront at a later time. Verify, using Huygen's principle, Snell's law of refraction of a plane wave propagating from a denser to a rarer medium. Illustrate with the help of diagrams the action of convex lens and concave mirror, on a plane wavefront incident on it.
explanation: The surface of constant phase is known as a wavefront. The Geometrical construction of wavefront: To determine the wavefront at t = {tex}\tau{/tex} draw spheres of radius v {tex}\tau{/tex} from each point on F 1 F 2 and draw a common tangent to these spheres to obtain the new position of the wavefront. The ratio of the speed of light in vaccum to the speed of light in the medium is termed as refractive index of medium. Let us consider the medium I, which is optically denser than medium 2. Let the speed of light be v 1 in medium I and v 2 in medium II. Always, note that v 2 > v 1 A plane wave AB propagates and hits the interface at an angle i. and can be the time taken be the wavefront to travel the distance BC. Now, we want to draw the refracted wavefront. We can draw a sphere of radius v 2 with A as centre. Let the surface tangent to the sphere passing through point C, as the refracted wavefront. Now, Let the surface be tangent to the sphere at E. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC sin i = {tex}\frac{v_1 t}{A C}{/tex} and, In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AEC {tex}\iota{/tex} sin r = {tex}\frac{v_2 t}{A C}{/tex} On dividing both the equations, we finally have, {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v_1}{v_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{c}{v_2}}{\frac{c}{v_1}}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This is the verified Snell's law.

question_html:
1. State the essential conditions for diffraction of light.
  2. Explain the diffraction of light due to a narrow single slit and the formation of pattern of fringes on the screen.
  3. Find the relation for the width of central maxima in terms of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, the width of slit a, and separation between slit and screen D.
  4. If the width of the slit is made double the original width, how does it affect the size and intensity of the central band?
question_text: State the essential conditions for diffraction of light. Explain the diffraction of light due to a narrow single slit and the formation of pattern of fringes on the screen. Find the relation for the width of central maxima in terms of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} , the width of slit a, and separation between slit and screen D. If the width of the slit is made double the original width, how does it affect the size and intensity of the central band?
explanation: Condition for diffraction of light is given by: Size of the slit/aperture must be smaller than of the order of wavelength of light. Single slit diffraction is explained by treating different parts of the wave front as the sources of secondary wavelets. Hence, At the central point C on the screen, {tex}\theta{/tex} is zero. Hence, All path differences are zero and all the parts of the slit contribute in phase and give maximum intensity at C. At any other point P, the path difference between two edges of the slit is given by: NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta=a \theta\because\sin \theta{/tex} is small Any point P, is direction {tex}\theta{/tex} , is a location of minima if a {tex}\theta{/tex} = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} This can be explained by dividing the slit into an even number of parts. The path difference between waves from successive parts is 180° out of phase and hence cancel each leading to a minimum. Any point P, in direction Q, is a location of maxima if {tex}a \theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} This can be explained by dividing the slit into an odd number of parts. The contributions from successive parts cancel in pairs because of 180° phase difference. The unpaired part produces intensity at P, leading to a maximum. If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the direction of first minima, then {tex}a \theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of central maxima is given by = 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{\alpha}{/tex} Linear width of central maxima is given by, {tex}\beta=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}=2 \theta \times D{/tex} If 'a' is doubled, {tex}\beta{/tex} becomes half. Hence, Intensity becomes 4 times.

question_html:
Two harmonic waves of monochromatic light
y1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t and y2 = a cos({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex}) 
are superimposed on each other. Show that maximum intensity in interference pattern is four times the intensity due to each slit. Hence write the conditions for constructive and destructive interference in terms of the phase angle {tex}\phi{/tex}.
question_text: Two harmonic waves of monochromatic light y 1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t and y 2 = a cos( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) are superimposed on each other. Show that maximum intensity in interference pattern is four times the intensity due to each slit. Hence write the conditions for constructive and destructive interference in terms of the phase angle {tex}\phi{/tex} .
explanation: The resultant displacement will be given by y = y 1 + y 2 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) = a[cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} )] = 2a cos ( {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) The amplitude of the resultant displacement is 2 a cos ( {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) The intensity of light is directly proportional to the square of amplitude of the wave. The resultant intensity will be given by I = 4a 2 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} , where I 0 = a 2 is the intensity of each harmonic wave At the maxima, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2n {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 1 At the maxima, I = 4I 0 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} intensity due to one slit I = 4I 0 cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) For constructive interference, I is maximum. It is possible when cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) = 1; {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = n {tex}\pi{/tex} ; {tex}\pi{/tex} = 2n {tex}\pi{/tex} For destructive interference, I is minimum, i.e., I = 0 It is possible when cos 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} = 0; {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 n-1) \pi}{2}{/tex} ; {tex}\phi{/tex} = (2n {tex}\pm{/tex} 1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution: The resultant displacement will be given by y = y 1 + y 2 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) = a[cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} )] = 2a cos ( {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) The amplitude of the resultant displacement is 2 a cos ( {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) The intensity of light is directly proportional to the square of amplitude of the wave. The resultant intensity will be given by I = 4a 2 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} , where I 0 = a 2 is the intensity of each harmonic wave At the maxima, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2n {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 1 At the maxima, I = 4I 0 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} intensity due to one slit I = 4I 0 cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) For constructive interference, I is maximum. It is possible when cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} ) = 1; {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = n {tex}\pi{/tex} ; {tex}\pi{/tex} = 2n {tex}\pi{/tex} For destructive interference, I is minimum, i.e., I = 0 It is possible when cos 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} = 0; {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(2 n-1) \pi}{2}{/tex} ; {tex}\phi{/tex} = (2n {tex}\pm{/tex} 1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

The resultant displacement will be given by
y = y1 + y2
= a cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t + a cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex})
= a[cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t + cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex})]
= 2a cos ({tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}) cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex})
The amplitude of the resultant displacement is 2 a cos ({tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex})
The intensity of light is directly proportional to the square of amplitude of the wave. The resultant intensity will be given by
I = 4a2 cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity = 4I0 cos2{tex}\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex}, where I0 = a2 is the intensity of each harmonic wave
At the maxima, {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex}2n{tex}\pi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} cos2{tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 1
At the maxima, I = 4I0 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} intensity due to one slit
I = 4I0 cos2({tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex})
For constructive interference, I is maximum.
It is possible when cos2({tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}) = 1; {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = n{tex}\pi{/tex}; {tex}\pi{/tex} = 2n{tex}\pi{/tex}
